description: Q-ATT&CK: Adversarial Tactics, Techniques and Common Knowledge for Quantum Cloud Infrastructure

tactics.name: Reconnaissance
tactics.description: Adversary gathers information about quantum cloud platform topology, hardware backends, calibration data, API versioning, and user account structures without triggering security alerts.

tactics.name: Resource Development
tactics.description: Adversary establishes resources to support quantum cloud operations including obtaining API credentials, developing malicious circuit payloads, and acquiring quantum compute budget.

tactics.name: Initial Access
tactics.description: Adversary gains entry to quantum cloud platform through credential theft, OAuth misconfiguration, SDK supply chain compromise, or API key exposure in public repositories.

tactics.name: Execution
tactics.description: Adversary submits malicious or probing quantum circuits, manipulates job queues, or abuses circuit compilation pipelines to achieve objectives on the quantum cloud platform.

tactics.name: Discovery
tactics.description: Adversary enumerates available quantum backends, infers hardware topology from calibration APIs, maps multi-tenant boundaries, and identifies co-tenants via timing analysis.

tactics.name: Collection
tactics.description: Adversary extracts calibration data, hardware noise characteristics, other users' job metadata, circuit intellectual property, or result data from quantum cloud infrastructure.

tactics.name: Impact
tactics.description: Adversary disrupts quantum cloud operations through resource exhaustion, job interference, denial of quantum compute, or corruption of calibration state.

techniques.name: Backend topology enumeration
techniques.description: Adversary queries quantum cloud provider APIs to enumerate available hardware backends, their qubit counts, connectivity graphs, and operational status. This information aids in identifying high-value shared backends and understanding physical qubit layouts that may be exploited for co-tenancy attacks.
detection_hints: Unusually high frequency of backend listing API calls
Enumeration of all available backends in short time window
Backend queries not followed by job submission
mitigation_hints: Rate-limit backend enumeration endpoints
Require authentication for detailed topology data
Aggregate backend queries in audit logs

techniques.name: Calibration data harvesting
techniques.description: Adversary systematically retrieves hardware calibration data (T1/T2 times, gate fidelities, readout errors, cross-talk matrices) to build detailed models of physical qubit behavior. Calibration fingerprints can identify specific hardware units, infer scheduling patterns, and support side-channel analysis.
detection_hints: Repeated calibration API calls across multiple backends
Calibration queries without corresponding quantum job submission
Automated calibration data export patterns
mitigation_hints: Limit calibration data granularity for free-tier users
Audit calibration data access frequency per user
Consider calibration data as sensitive infrastructure information

techniques.name: Job queue timing analysis
techniques.description: Adversary submits minimal probe circuits and measures execution delay patterns to infer queue depth, other users' job sizes, and scheduling algorithms. Timing oracles in shared quantum backends can reveal information about co-tenants' computational workloads.
detection_hints: High frequency of minimal single-gate circuit submissions
Pattern of submission followed immediately by status polling
Systematic variation of circuit depth with timing measurement
mitigation_hints: Add timing jitter to queue position information
Limit job status polling frequency
Avoid exposing queue depth in API responses

techniques.name: API key exposure via public repository
techniques.description: Adversary searches public code repositories (GitHub, GitLab, HuggingFace) for exposed IBM Quantum tokens, AWS credentials, or other quantum cloud API keys accidentally committed by developers. Qiskit configuration files (qiskitrc) and Jupyter notebooks are common exposure vectors.
detection_hints: API calls from unusual geographic locations
Sudden increase in compute usage on existing account
Access from IP addresses not associated with account history
mitigation_hints: Implement credential scanning in CI/CD pipelines
Rotate API keys on any public repository exposure
Use short-lived tokens where platform supports them
Monitor for qiskitrc files in public repositories

techniques.name: OAuth scope misconfiguration abuse
techniques.description: Adversary exploits overly broad OAuth scopes granted during quantum cloud platform authentication to access resources beyond intended permissions. Platforms offering programmatic access via OAuth may not enforce principle of least privilege on scope definitions.
detection_hints: Token requests for maximum available scopes
Access to administrative endpoints using user-level OAuth tokens
Scope escalation attempts in token refresh flows
mitigation_hints: Enforce scope validation on every API call, not just token issuance
Implement fine-grained scopes (read:backends, submit:jobs, read:results)
Audit token scopes in access logs

techniques.name: SDK supply chain compromise
techniques.description: Adversary compromises quantum SDK packages (Qiskit, Braket SDK, Cirq) via typosquatting on PyPI, dependency confusion attacks, or malicious pull requests to extract credentials, exfiltrate circuit intellectual property, or redirect job results to adversary-controlled endpoints.
detection_hints: Unexpected network connections from SDK installation
Hash mismatch on installed SDK packages
SDK packages installed from non-official registries
mitigation_hints: Pin SDK dependencies to verified hash values
Use private package registries with integrity verification
Audit SDK network behavior in sandboxed environments

techniques.name: Calibration-state pollution circuit
techniques.description: Adversary submits carefully crafted circuits designed to degrade hardware calibration state, increasing error rates for subsequent users' jobs on shared backends. By exploiting the physical nature of qubit decoherence, an adversary may be able to cause targeted computation failures for co-tenants.
detection_hints: Circuits with unusually high gate counts targeting specific qubits
Systematic submission to same backend repeatedly
Circuit patterns inconsistent with legitimate quantum algorithms
mitigation_hints: Implement circuit analysis before execution
Monitor per-qubit error rate drift across jobs
Isolate calibration cycles between tenant jobs on sensitive backends

techniques.name: Resource exhaustion via deep circuit submission
techniques.description: Adversary submits circuits at or near maximum allowed depth and qubit count to exhaust quantum compute budget allocations, occupy hardware backends, and deny service to legitimate users. Free-tier rate limits may be insufficient to prevent queue flooding.
detection_hints: Sustained submission of maximum-depth circuits
Queue occupancy spike from single user
Job submission rate at rate limit ceiling
mitigation_hints: Implement per-user queue depth limits
Priority queuing for paid tiers
Anomaly detection on job submission patterns

techniques.name: Multi-tenant boundary probing
techniques.description: Adversary submits circuits specifically designed to detect the presence of other users' computations on shared quantum hardware, attempting to identify co-tenancy through error rate variation, qubit availability patterns, or cross-talk signatures that change with neighboring qubit usage.
detection_hints: Circuits designed to measure cross-talk on idle qubits
Repeated identical circuit submissions with comparison of noise characteristics
Circuits targeting qubit pairs known for high cross-talk
mitigation_hints: Physical qubit partitioning between concurrent users where possible
Noise characterization results should not be tenant-distinguishable
Randomize qubit assignment to prevent fingerprinting

techniques.name: Backend hardware fingerprinting
techniques.description: Adversary uses calibration data and noise characteristics to fingerprint specific physical quantum processor units, enabling tracking of which specific hardware unit is being used across sessions and correlating this with known vulnerability profiles of specific devices.
detection_hints: Systematic collection of noise characteristics across multiple backends
Correlation of calibration data with job execution timing
Backend selection patterns inconsistent with performance optimization
mitigation_hints: Obfuscate backend-specific calibration signatures in API responses
Add calibration data noise for anonymization
Do not expose hardware unit serial identifiers in API

techniques.name: Circuit intellectual property extraction
techniques.description: Adversary with platform administrative access or through API misconfiguration attempts to access other users' submitted circuit designs, which may represent proprietary quantum algorithms, pharmaceutical molecular simulations, or financial optimization models with significant commercial value.
detection_hints: API calls accessing job data outside of authenticated user's namespace
IDOR attempts on job ID endpoints
Bulk job listing across tenant boundaries
mitigation_hints: Strict object-level authorization on all job endpoints
Encrypt circuit data at rest with per-tenant keys
Audit log all job data access

techniques.name: Result leakage via shared backend state
techniques.description: Adversary exploits residual quantum state or measurement correlation artifacts on shared quantum hardware to infer partial information about previous users' circuit results. While difficult in practice with current error rates, this represents a novel threat class unique to multi-tenant quantum computing.
detection_hints: Circuits specifically designed to measure residual state from reset operations
Systematic comparison of fresh-initialized vs reused qubit behavior
Statistical analysis of measurement results for non-random patterns
mitigation_hints: Implement verified qubit reset protocols between tenant jobs
Research and publish reset fidelity guarantees
Consider dedicated qubit allocation for sensitive computations

techniques.name: Account structure enumeration
techniques.description: Adversary probes quantum cloud platform APIs for information about account structures, organization hierarchies, group memberships, and user identifiers to map the tenant landscape and identify high-value accounts for targeting.
detection_hints: Enumeration of organization or group endpoints
Sequential or pattern-based user ID probing
API calls to account management endpoints by non-administrative users
mitigation_hints: Implement rate limiting on account enumeration endpoints
Return consistent responses for valid and invalid account identifiers
Restrict organization membership visibility

techniques.name: Quantum compute budget theft
techniques.description: Adversary uses stolen credentials or abuses platform trial credits to consume quantum compute resources at scale, either for their own quantum computation needs or to conduct extended attack campaigns requiring many circuit submissions.
detection_hints: Sudden spike in compute usage on compromised account
Job submission from unusual geographic location
Credit consumption rate inconsistent with historical account behavior
mitigation_hints: Implement anomaly detection on credit consumption
Geographic access controls or MFA step-up for large compute requests
Real-time alerting on unusual usage spikes

techniques.name: Transpiler information disclosure
techniques.description: Adversary submits circuits to cloud transpiler endpoints and analyzes the transpiled output to infer hardware topology details, qubit connectivity, and gate set characteristics that are not directly exposed via API but are revealed through transpilation behavior.
detection_hints: High volume of transpile-only requests without execution
Systematic variation of input circuits to probe transpiler behavior
Transpile requests targeting unavailable or restricted backends
mitigation_hints: Rate-limit transpilation-only API calls
Consider hardware topology data as sensitive and limit indirect disclosure
Audit transpilation patterns in access logs

techniques.name: Quantum job interference
techniques.description: Adversary with access to job management APIs attempts to cancel, modify, or delay other users' quantum jobs through authorization bypass vulnerabilities in job lifecycle management endpoints. Successful execution could disrupt time-sensitive quantum computations.
detection_hints: Job cancellation API calls for job IDs not owned by authenticated user
IDOR probing on job management endpoints
Job status modification attempts on foreign job IDs
mitigation_hints: Strict object-level authorization on all job lifecycle endpoints
Separate authentication context for job management vs result retrieval
Audit all job cancellation events with user attribution

techniques.name: Jupyter notebook credential exposure
techniques.description: Adversary targets public Jupyter notebooks on GitHub, Kaggle, Google Colab, and HuggingFace that contain hardcoded IBM Quantum tokens, AWS credentials, or Azure service principal credentials used for quantum computing demonstrations and tutorials. This is the highest-frequency credential exposure vector in the quantum ecosystem.
detection_hints: Credential use from IP addresses inconsistent with account owner location
API access patterns inconsistent with Jupyter interactive usage
Bulk operations not typical of interactive notebook sessions
mitigation_hints: Scan all published notebooks for credential patterns before publication
Use environment variables and secrets managers in notebooks
IBM Quantum should implement token usage monitoring for unusual access patterns
Implement notebook scanning in GitHub Actions for qiskitrc and token patterns
